filename: biodiversity-land/Forests and Land - Our Most Important Carbon Sink.md
label: Forests and Land: Our Most Important Carbon Sink
summary: Forests absorb roughly 30% of the carbon humans emit each year, making them one of the most important tools we have against climate change. But that role is under serious threat. In 2024, tropical forest loss nearly doubled compared to the previous year, driven by fires, drought and agricultural expansion. Some forests are already becoming net carbon emitters rather than absorbers. Mixed forests outperform monocultures significantly, capturing up to 25% more carbon. The priority is clear: protect what exists first, then restore. Reforestation helps, but it cannot replace primary forests that took centuries to develop.

The footwear industry produces nearly 24 billion pairs a year, accounts for 1.4% of global carbon emissions, and sends 95% of used shoes to landfill. label: The Circular Economy: A Different Way of Thinking About Stuff
summary: The circular economy offers an alternative to the linear model of take, make, dispose, keeping products and materials in use for as long as possible. It is a central pillar of the EU's climate strategy, yet progress has been slow. The EU's circularity rate stood at just 12.2% in 2024, against a 2030 target of 24%. New legislation, including the Right to Repair Directive, rules on textile and food waste, and the forthcoming Circular Economy Act, is pushing the transition forward. But financing gaps, uneven progress across member states, and persistent reliance on linear models mean the hard work is still ahead.

label: Climate Adaptation and Resilience: Why It Matters and What Is at Stake
summary: Climate change is already reshaping the world we live in, and reducing emissions alone is not enough. Alongside mitigation, societies must adapt to impacts that are now unavoidable. This article explores two key concepts: adaptation, which refers to the concrete actions taken in response to climate risks, and resilience, which describes the capacity of a system to absorb and recover from shocks. Resilience is never universal. It depends on who is affected, what type of stress is involved, and over what timeframe. Building resilience for today's risks can sometimes create new vulnerabilities tomorrow. Food systems illustrate this challenge particularly well. Responsible for up to 37% of global greenhouse gas emissions, and increasingly threatened by drought, heat, and extreme weather, food systems sit at the heart of the climate adaptation challenge. Crop yields are already falling, hunger is rising again after decades of progress, and the most exposed regions, particularly in South and Southeast Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa, have the fewest resources to respond.

filename: climate-finance/Climate Change and Financial Risk - What Is at Stake.md
label: Climate Change and Financial Risk: What Is at Stake
summary: Climate change is not only an environmental crisis. It is a financial one. The World Economic Forum ranks climate-related failures among the top global risks of the next decade, and the numbers back that up. The Swiss Re Institute estimates the global economy could lose up to 18% of GDP by 2050 if warming continues unchecked. Climate risk takes two main forms: physical risk, from the direct damage caused by floods, droughts, and extreme weather, and transition risk, from the financial disruption that occurs as economies move away from fossil fuels. Both are already materialising. Insured losses from natural disasters reached 115 billion dollars in 2022, well above the ten-year average. Meanwhile, trillions of dollars in fossil fuel assets face the prospect of losing their value as clean energy becomes cheaper and climate policy tightens. Despite this, the world's 60 largest banks provided 673 billion dollars in fossil fuel financing in 2022 alone. This article examines both types of risk, the sectors most exposed, and the organisations working to bring greater transparency and accountability to climate risk in finance.

filename: climate-finance/Climate and Finance - Why Money Is at the Heart of the Climate Crisis.md
label: Climate and Finance: Why Money Is at the Heart of the Climate Crisis
summary: Finance sits at the heart of the climate crisis, yet it rarely gets the attention it deserves in public debate. Addressing climate change at the scale required will cost trillions of dollars every year, and neither governments nor private investors are currently deploying capital fast enough. At the same time, the world's largest banks and financial institutions continue to direct enormous sums toward fossil fuels, often while publicly committing to net zero. This article explores the full landscape of climate finance: how public and private money flows today, where the gaps are, and where progress is being made. It covers key instruments including green bonds, ESG investing, debt-for-climate swaps, and venture capital, alongside their limitations and the persistent problem of greenwashing. It also looks at what individuals can do, because personal banking and investment choices have a more direct climate impact than most people realise. Finance can be a powerful lever for climate action. Whether it becomes one depends on the choices made by governments, institutions, and ordinary people alike.

filename: climate-finance/Is your Bank Is Funding Climate Change. Here Is What You Can Do About It..md
label: Is your Bank Funding Climate Change? Here Is What You Can Do About It.
summary: Most people trying to reduce their climate impact think about flights, diet, or energy use at home. But one of the most powerful actions available is one that almost nobody talks about: choosing where to bank. Banks use your deposits to fund loans and investments across the economy, and a significant share of that money flows to fossil fuel companies and other carbon-intensive industries. Research by Project Drawdown shows that every 1,000 euros held in a carbon-intensive bank generates roughly the same emissions as a return transatlantic flight, every year. Switching to a greener bank could reduce the climate impact of your personal banking by up to 76%. It is a one-time action that requires no ongoing effort and no financial sacrifice. This article explains how the banking and climate connection works, what to look for in a greener bank, and where to find independent tools to help you make the switch.

label: What the Latest IPCC Report Tells Us: Ten Findings That Matter
summary: n March 2023, the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change published the final part of its Sixth Assessment Report, the most authoritative scientific review of climate change ever produced. Drawing on the work of more than 780 scientists, its conclusions are unambiguous: the situation is worse than previously assessed, and the window for meaningful action is closing fast. The planet has already warmed by 1.1 degrees Celsius, and the impacts on people and ecosystems are more severe and widespread than earlier models predicted. Staying within 1.5 degrees of warming requires global emissions to peak before 2025 and fall by 43% by 2030. That means a rapid phase-out of fossil fuels, deep cuts across all sectors of the economy, and significant deployment of carbon removal. It also requires a dramatic increase in climate finance, particularly for adaptation in developing countries, which currently receive a fraction of what they need. And it requires recognising that without deliberate action, climate change will deepen existing inequalities, hitting the poorest communities hardest despite their having contributed least to the problem.

label: Mental Models for Climate Solutions
summary: Climate solutions are not just a technical question. They are also a question of how we think. This article introduces four mental models that shape how experts and decision-makers evaluate and compare solutions: a cost and impact framework, the growth versus degrowth debate, a systems approach that looks at multiple problems together, and a reflective lens that asks not just what to do but how to do it well. Understanding these models, and their blind spots, is essential before deciding where to invest time, money or policy attention.

filename: concepts/behavior-change/Talking about Climate Change ACTION GUIDE.md
label: Talking about Climate Change - Climate Advocacy
summary: Never doubt that a small group of thoughtful, committed, citizens can change the world. Indeed, it is the only thing that ever has.' - Margaret Mead, The Need for Conversations, Leading by Example, Taking action and being a positive role model are crucial to ensuring our conversations about climate change are more than just words. When we embody the change we want to see, our actions lend weight to our words, making them far more persuasive, More Science is Not the Answer, Knowing Your Audience, Using the Right Words, Changing how we talk about climate change can make a big difference. Using clear, positive language can inspire action and support for policies to address the crisis urgently. Instead of focusing on sacrifice, we should highlight the opportunities for better health and well-being in a world moving beyond fossil fuels. Avoid scientific jargon and misleading terms. For example, 'natural disasters' are often influenced by human actions, making them not entirely natural. Clear communication can help shift perceptions and motivate collective action towards a sustainable future, Susan Joy Hassol shares an illustrative list in her article The Right Words Are Crucial to Solving Climate Change, Ignore the Outliers, Focus your efforts on the majority open to taking action, rather than trying to convert the dismissive few. Campaigns often target extremes, but targeting the middle 50% under the proverbial curve is where our efforts can make the biggest difference. Engaging the willing majority can build the political will and community support needed for meaningful climate action, Communicating Solutions, Highlighting solutions is as important as identifying problems. Focusing only on problems can overwhelm people, making them disengage. Amanda Ripley highlights the need for hope, agency, and dignity in climate coverage in her article in the Washington Post. Most stories miss discussing solutions, leaving audiences feeling powerless. Highlighting positive steps and celebrating small victories can inspire action. It's important to balance fear with actionable hope and not to overemphasize sacrifice, Showcase exciting aspects of climate action, like the benefits of electric vehicles, induction cooking, and vegetarian diets. Heating is one of Ireland's most overlooked climate problems. It accounts for nearly a quarter of all national greenhouse gas emissions, it is still dominated by oil and fossil fuels, and emissions from the sector have actually been rising since 2014. The SEAI National Heat Study, commissioned by the Sustainable Energy Authority of Ireland, provides the most comprehensive analysis to date of what it will take to decarbonise heating by 2050. The findings are clear: current policy is not enough, and the pace of change needs to accelerate significantly.

filename: sectors/energy/Waste to Energy -Why Burning Rubbish Might Not a Climate Solution**.md
label: Waste to Energy: Why Burning Rubbish Might Not a Climate Solution
summary: Waste to energy plants burn waste to generate electricity and heat. The concept seems practical, but Project Drawdown rates it as "not recommended" as a climate solution. Incineration produces greenhouse gas emissions, is less efficient than most alternatives, and creates a structural dependency on waste production that runs counter to circular economy principles. Some EU countries already import waste to keep their plants running. Air pollution and environmental justice concerns add to the problems. Pyrolysis and gasification, which convert waste using heat without direct combustion, perform significantly better. Incineration may have a residual role for hazardous waste, but it is not a credible path to emissions reduction.

label: Carbon Removal: Companies to Watch
summary: Technologie - Carbon removal is a growing field, but it is still far from the scale needed to make a real difference. This article looks at four examples of companies and research projects working on it, using very different approaches. Terra Fertilis uses biochar to store carbon in soil while improving its health. Silicate, an Irish start-up, spreads cement dust on farmland to trigger natural mineral absorption of CO2. Heirloom Carbon pulls CO2 directly from the air using processed limestone. A separate research team is developing an electrically charged mist to capture carbon from power plant emissions. Each approach has promise, and each has limits.

filename: solutions/nature-based-solutions/Grazing Management as a Nature-Based Climate Solution -  What the Science Really Says.md
label: Grazing Management as a Nature-Based Climate Solution: What the Science Really Says
summary: Managed grazing covers approaches like rotational grazing and reduced stocking density that aim to improve soil health and store more carbon in grassland soils. Because livestock grazing covers roughly 25% of all land on Earth, better management of that land has real climate potential. Project Drawdown recognises this, but currently places both alternative grazing and reduced grazing intensity in a "Keep Watching" category. Evidence of consistent soil carbon gains is limited, results vary significantly by climate and soil type, and methane from livestock remains an unresolved issue. Managed grazing is a useful tool, but not a standalone climate solution.

label: The Importance of Hedgerows
summary: Hedgerows are a powerful nature-based solution, storing carbon, supporting biodiversity, improving water quality, and protecting farmland. The EU Biodiversity Strategy and CAP 2023–27 target at least 10% of agricultural land under landscape features including hedgerows, as part of broader climate goals. In Ireland, around 689,000 km of hedgerows exist but are declining, with the Climate Action Plan 2024 targeting 40,000 km of new planting. France faces similar pressure, having lost over 40% of its hedgerows since the 1970s. Its 2023 Hedgerow Pact aims for a net gain of 50,000 km by 2030.

filename: solutions/nature-based-solutions/The incredible environmental properties of Bamboo.md
label: Bamboo: a versatile material for the low-carbon transition
summary: Bamboo plantations have incredible environmental properties: with their expansive underground stems, they reduce soil erosion and prevent sediments from entering streams and rivers. Fallen leaves carpet the ground, which reduces water evaporation. Their hollow stems retain water into the dry season, thus increasing environmental humidity. Dense bamboo vegetation also captures water droplets from fog. It is one of the top 100 solutions proposed by Project Drawdown in order to stay under 1.5C

B Corp Certification: What It Is, How It Works, and Why It Matters
summary: B Corp certification is awarded by B Lab, a global non-profit, to companies that meet verified standards across governance, workers, community, environment, and customers. Companies must score at least 80 out of 200 on the B Impact Assessment, pass a verification review, and embed stakeholder accountability into their legal structure. Certification must be renewed every three years. Over 10,800 companies across 102 countries are now certified, including global brands such as Patagonia and Innocent Drinks. In Ireland, the movement has grown rapidly since its first certification in 2018, with over 90 companies now certified and state funding available to support the process through Enterprise Ireland.

filename: standards-labels/Ecobalyse is a new eco-score for Food and Fashion.md
label: Ecobalyse: France's Environmental Score for Products
summary: Ecobalyse is a free, open-source tool developed by the French government to calculate the environmental impact of products sold in France. It uses life cycle assessment methodology across 16 indicators, including carbon emissions, water use, and microplastic pollution, to produce a single "environmental cost" score. Currently focused on clothing and food, it is the official reference tool under France's 2021 Climate and Resilience Law. From October 2026, any third party can publish a score on behalf of a brand that has not calculated its own. The tool is also being watched at European level as a potential model for EU-wide textile labelling by 2030.

label: EcoVadis: A Sustainability Rating for Your Supply Chain
summary: What is Ecovadis, Ecovadis certifications, The Process, Medals, The Subscriptions, EcoVadis methodology. EcoVadis is a sustainability rating platform used by companies worldwide to assess and improve ESG performance across their supply chains. It covers four areas: environment, labour rights, ethics, and sustainable procurement. Companies complete a questionnaire, submit supporting documents, and receive a score out of 100 reviewed by EcoVadis analysts. High-performing companies are awarded a Bronze, Silver, Gold, or Platinum medal, reflecting their position relative to others in the database. The rating is not a product certification, but a measure of management quality. It is widely required by large buyers as part of supplier qualification, making it a practical standard for supply chain due diligence.

filename: standards-labels/The MSC Blue Label - What It Means and Why It Matters.md
label: The MSC Blue Label: What It Means and Why It Matters
summary: The Marine Stewardship Council (MSC) is a non-profit that certifies wild seafood fisheries against a set of environmental sustainability standards covering fish stock health, ecosystem impact, and fisheries management. Its distinctive blue label appears on over 20,000 products worldwide. Certification is carried out by independent third-party auditors, not the MSC itself, and involves scoring across 28 performance indicators. Fisheries are audited annually and must recertify every five years. The MSC label does not cover social conditions or carbon footprint, and has faced some criticism over conflicts of interest. It remains the most widely recognised and adopted standard for sustainable wild-catch seafood globally.

label: The TNFD: What It Is, Who Joined, and What Comes Next
summary: The Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures (TNFD) was created to help companies and financial institutions identify and report on risks related to nature, including biodiversity loss, water stress, and deforestation. Published in 2023, its framework covers governance, strategy, risk management, and targets. Adoption grew rapidly, with over 700 companies representing 9.4 trillion dollars in market value signing on across 54 countries. In November 2025, the TNFD announced it will close by 2026 and transfer responsibility for nature reporting standards to the International Sustainability Standards Board (ISSB), with final standards expected by 2027. Nature disclosure is becoming mainstream corporate reporting.
